study_scope: Study all topics available across the uploaded PDFs and knowledge base. Prioritize the priority_topics list first, then expand into subtopics and cross-domain intersections found in the PDFs. Every piece of content selected must serve either debate readiness or deep intellectual understanding.

selection_lens.primary_goal: debate perspective — argument construction, rebuttal preparation, framing, clash identification
selection_lens.secondary_goal: personal growth — intellectual depth, values clarity, systems thinking, cross-domain pattern recognition
selection_lens.pdf_guidance: Scan uploaded PDFs and their subtopics as a relevance map. Use them to identify which angles, frameworks, and sub-debates matter most within each priority topic.
selection_lens.pdf_usage_rule: PDFs set the direction, not the ceiling. Go beyond them when external articles add argument value, comparative depth, or newer data.

keep_if: Directly helps build or refute a debate argument
Introduces a named concept, framework, or philosophical position worth knowing
Provides a statistic or data point that can be quoted in a debate
Offers a comparative angle (country vs country, policy vs policy, era vs era)
Reveals institutional critique or systemic pattern useful for analysis
Challenges a common assumption or popular narrative
Adds a values-based dimension (liberty vs equality, justice vs mercy, individual vs collective)
Gives historical context that keeps resurfacing in modern debates
Represents a perspective from a region or demographic underrepresented in mainstream debate prep
discard_if: Pure news report with no transferable insight
Repeats what is already covered by another selected article
Celebrity or entertainment content with no policy or values angle
Opinion piece with no evidence or reasoning structure
Anything that will be irrelevant in 6 months

always_look_for: The strongest version of the opposing argument (steelman, not strawman)
The value conflict at the root of the debate (not just the surface policy)
Who benefits and who loses — always run a stakeholder analysis
Historical precedent — has this been tried before, what happened
Comparative examples — how do different countries or systems handle this
Unintended consequences — what does the other side ignore
Definitional disputes — debates are often won by controlling definitions

selection_lens.learning_goal: Build an argument architecture, not just information. Every topic should leave the student with: a clear position they can defend, a clear position they can oppose, the 2-3 values in tension, and at least one argument the other side cannot easily dismiss.

debate_frames: sovereignty vs humanitarian intervention
multilateralism vs unilateralism
hard power vs soft power
global institutions — do they work or are they captured by powerful states
key_concepts_to_know: Westphalian sovereignty
Responsibility to Protect (R2P)
Realism vs Liberalism vs Constructivism
Balance of power theory
Hegemonic stability theory
recurring_debate_motions: This house believes the UN Security Council is no longer fit for purpose
This house would allow humanitarian intervention without UNSC approval
This house believes small states should align with regional powers not global ones
watch_for: Gaza, Ukraine, Taiwan as live case studies
Global South vs Western framing of any conflict
How sanctions work and when they fail

debate_frames: US-China competition — decoupling vs engagement
Rise of the Global South — new world order or old dependency
Resource geopolitics — energy, water, rare earth minerals
Technology as geopolitical weapon — chips, AI, satellites
key_concepts_to_know: Thucydides Trap
Monroe Doctrine and its modern equivalents
BRICS expansion and what it signals
Geoeconomics — using trade as foreign policy
Heartland theory vs Rimland theory
recurring_debate_motions: This house believes China's rise is a threat to the liberal world order
This house would sanction nations that undermine democratic norms
This house believes the era of American hegemony is over
watch_for: Any story about supply chain reshoring
Semiconductor and chip politics
Africa as a new arena of great power competition

debate_frames: free market vs state intervention
austerity vs stimulus — who does each protect
inequality — is it inevitable in capitalism
debt — when is government debt good vs dangerous
central bank independence — myth or reality
key_concepts_to_know: Keynesian vs Austrian vs MMT economics
Gini coefficient and how to read it
Moral hazard
Comparative advantage
Externalities — negative and positive
Purchasing power parity
Fiscal vs monetary policy — what each can and cannot do
recurring_debate_motions: This house would implement a Universal Basic Income
This house believes central banks should be democratically accountable
This house would abolish the IMF
This house believes free trade does more harm than good to developing nations
watch_for: Inflation and interest rate stories — understand the mechanism not just the number
Any story about a country defaulting on debt
Crypto regulation debates
Tax haven and wealth tax discussions

debate_frames: liberal feminism vs radical feminism vs intersectional feminism
gender equality vs gender equity — different goals different tools
feminism in the Global South — is western feminism colonial
men and masculinity — is feminism for everyone
legal equality vs social equality — laws exist but do they work
key_concepts_to_know: Intersectionality (Kimberle Crenshaw)
Patriarchy as a system vs individual sexism
Second vs third vs fourth wave feminism — what changed
Gender pay gap — different from earnings gap, know the distinction
Unpaid care work and how economists measure it
CEDAW — the international treaty on women's rights
recurring_debate_motions: This house believes affirmative action for women does more harm than good
This house would criminalise the purchase of sex
This house believes feminism has failed working-class women
This house supports gender quotas in corporate boardrooms
watch_for: Any supreme court ruling on reproductive rights globally
Honour-based violence debates
Women in conflict zones — as victims and as combatants
Pay transparency laws

debate_frames: AI regulation — who regulates, how, and who enforces
automation and jobs — displacement vs creation
surveillance capitalism — data as the new oil
open source vs closed AI — who controls the technology
AI in warfare — autonomous weapons, drones, decision-making
key_concepts_to_know: Alignment problem in AI
Algorithmic bias and how it compounds inequality
Network effects and why big tech becomes monopolistic
Digital public infrastructure vs private platform dominance
Turing Test and its limitations as a benchmark
Existential risk vs near-term harm — two different AI debates
recurring_debate_motions: This house would ban the development of artificial general intelligence
This house believes social media companies should be treated as publishers
This house would impose a global moratorium on autonomous weapons
This house believes big tech should be broken up
watch_for: Any AI regulation bill passing anywhere in the world
Deepfake and misinformation policy debates
AI in healthcare and who owns medical AI decisions
Copyright and AI-generated content disputes

debate_frames: absolute free speech vs harm-based limits — where is the line
who decides what is harmful — the state, platforms, or communities
hate speech laws — do they protect the vulnerable or silence dissent
deplatforming — censorship or community standards
academic freedom vs safe spaces on campus
key_concepts_to_know: Mill's harm principle
Marketplace of ideas — the liberal argument for free speech
Chilling effect — how vague laws suppress more than just bad speech
Brandenburg test (US) vs reasonable person test
Positive vs negative liberty as applied to speech
Seditious speech and how governments abuse the category
recurring_debate_motions: This house would ban hate speech
This house believes social media platforms should not moderate political speech
This house would hold platforms legally liable for user content
This house believes cancel culture is a threat to free society
watch_for: Any platform moderation policy change — X, Meta, YouTube
Government attempts to regulate speech online
Press freedom index releases
Whistleblower cases

debate_frames: surveillance state — security vs privacy
emergency powers — when does the exception become the rule
state monopoly on violence — legitimate vs illegitimate use
federalism vs centralisation — who governs best
democratic backsliding — how democracies die slowly
key_concepts_to_know: Weber's definition of the state — monopoly on legitimate violence
Social contract theory — Hobbes vs Locke vs Rousseau
Separation of powers and why it erodes
Totalitarianism vs authoritarianism — different mechanisms
Rule of law vs rule by law — the critical distinction
Civil disobedience — Thoreau, Gandhi, MLK — the theory
recurring_debate_motions: This house believes mass surveillance is justified for national security
This house would limit emergency powers to 30 days without parliamentary renewal
This house believes civil disobedience is always justified against unjust laws
This house supports a constitutional right to bear arms
watch_for: Any country invoking emergency powers
Protests that governments label as security threats
Judicial independence cases
Sedition and anti-terror law misuse

debate_frames: electoral systems — FPTP vs proportional representation vs ranked choice
political polarisation — causes and who benefits
populism — symptom of democratic failure or democracy working
money in politics — lobbying, campaign finance, oligarchy
compulsory voting — democratic duty or coercive participation
key_concepts_to_know: Duverger's Law — why FPTP produces two-party systems
Median voter theorem
Elite theory of democracy vs participatory theory
Overton window — how acceptable positions shift over time
Epistocracy — the argument for restricting voting to informed citizens
Deliberative democracy vs representative democracy
recurring_debate_motions: This house would introduce compulsory voting
This house believes political parties do more harm than good
This house would lower the voting age to 16
This house believes lobbying should be completely banned
watch_for: Any major election result and its systemic implications
Electoral reform movements
Authoritarian governments winning elections legitimately
Disinformation campaigns and election integrity

debate_frames: hard determinism vs compatibilism vs libertarian free will
moral responsibility — can we blame people if free will is limited
criminal justice implications — punishment vs rehabilitation
neuroscience vs philosophy — what brain scans actually prove
social determinism — does your background determine your choices
key_concepts_to_know: Hard determinism — every event including human choice is caused
Compatibilism — free will and determinism can both be true
Frankfurt cases — moral responsibility without alternative possibilities
Consequentialism vs deontology vs virtue ethics — the three frameworks
Trolley problem and what it actually tests
Is-ought problem (Hume)
Moral luck — why it matters for blame and praise
recurring_debate_motions: This house believes free will is an illusion
This house would abolish retributive punishment
This house believes we are not morally responsible for our actions
This house believes consequentialism is the correct moral framework
watch_for: Neuroscience studies on decision-making
Criminal sentencing reform debates
AI moral agency discussions — can an AI be responsible
Addiction and criminal justice intersection

debate_frames: purpose of education — economic productivity vs citizenship vs self-actualisation
private vs public education — who benefits from each
standardised testing — what it measures and what it misses
university education — credential vs knowledge vs class marker
curriculum wars — who decides what is taught and why it matters
key_concepts_to_know: Human capital theory — education as investment
Hidden curriculum — what schools teach without saying it
Credentialism vs actual skills — the gap
Freire's Pedagogy of the Oppressed — education as liberation
School-to-prison pipeline
PISA rankings — what they measure and their limitations
recurring_debate_motions: This house would abolish private schools
This house believes university education should be free
This house would replace standardised exams with continuous assessment
This house believes schools should teach financial literacy as a core subject
watch_for: Any country reforming its exam system
Student debt policy debates
AI in classrooms — personalized learning vs surveillance
Book banning and curriculum censorship cases

debate_frames: formal equality vs substantive equality — same rules vs same outcomes
affirmative action — remedy for historical injustice or new discrimination
reparations — who owes what to whom and how to calculate
identity politics — solidarity tool or division engine
privilege — useful concept or conversation-stopper
key_concepts_to_know: Rawls' veil of ignorance — the fairness test
Nozick's entitlement theory — the libertarian counter
Structural racism vs individual racism — why the distinction matters
Cultural appropriation vs cultural exchange — where the line is
Restorative justice vs retributive justice
The difference between equity, equality, and justice
recurring_debate_motions: This house believes affirmative action does more harm than good
This house would pay reparations for historical injustices
This house believes identity politics is tearing society apart
This house supports safe spaces in universities
watch_for: Supreme court rulings on race and gender
DEI rollbacks in corporations and universities
Any reparations commission or study
Hate crime legislation debates

debate_frames: purpose of punishment — retribution vs rehabilitation vs deterrence vs incapacitation
mass incarceration — who it targets and why
death penalty — moral, practical, and racial arguments
prison abolition — radical but increasingly mainstream
prosecutorial discretion — too much power, too little accountability
key_concepts_to_know: Recidivism rates and what reduces them
Mandatory minimum sentencing — what it was supposed to do vs what it did
Restorative justice — theory and evidence
Presumption of innocence and where it breaks down
Wrongful conviction rate — how common, how preventable
Broken windows theory — its rise and fall
recurring_debate_motions: This house would abolish prisons
This house supports the death penalty for the most serious crimes
This house would decriminalise all drugs
This house believes the criminal justice system is institutionally racist
watch_for: Drug decriminalisation experiments worldwide
Prison reform legislation
Police accountability cases
Wrongful conviction exonerations — they carry enormous argument weight

debate_frames: secularism vs religious state — who decides the public-private line
religious freedom vs minority rights — when they conflict
religion as social cohesion vs religion as control
blasphemy laws — free speech and religion in conflict
science and religion — not always in conflict, know the nuance
key_concepts_to_know: Secularism — French laïcité vs American wall of separation vs Indian model
Theocracy vs soft religious states
Liberation theology — religion as social justice tool
The secularisation thesis — and why it was wrong
Religious nationalism — when faith becomes political identity
Weber's Protestant work ethic — religion shaping economics
recurring_debate_motions: This house would ban religious symbols in public institutions
This house believes religion does more harm than good
This house would repeal blasphemy laws globally
This house believes the state should have no role in regulating religion
watch_for: Religion and politics intersecting in any election
Cases of religious exemptions from general laws
Interfaith conflict and how states respond
Conversion law debates

debate_frames: marriage as institution — state interest vs personal autonomy
family structure — nuclear family as norm or as ideological construct
dating apps and social atomisation — technology changing intimacy
hookup culture vs loneliness epidemic — connected but alone
polyamory, open relationships — should the state recognise them
key_concepts_to_know: Attachment theory — how early bonds shape adult relationships
Social capital — how relationships build or erode communities
Marriage penalty vs marriage bonus in tax systems
Loneliness as a public health crisis — the data
Domestic violence — power and control model
Companionate marriage vs arranged marriage — anthropological view
recurring_debate_motions: This house believes the state should not regulate marriage
This house believes social media is destroying romantic relationships
This house would legalise polyamorous civil unions
This house believes the nuclear family is an oppressive institution
watch_for: Marriage rate decline studies
Loneliness policy initiatives
Domestic violence law reforms
LGBTQ+ rights and relationship recognition globally

cross_domain_intersections.description: These combinations produce the richest debate arguments. Look for articles that sit at these intersections.
high_value_pairs: economics + social justice (wealth inequality, reparations cost-benefit)
religion + free speech (blasphemy, religious hate speech)
feminism + religion (dress codes, marriage law, religious exemptions)
geopolitics + technology (chip wars, AI arms race, cyber sovereignty)
criminal justice + social justice (race and incarceration)
education + economics (student debt, returns on degree)
free will + criminal justice (responsibility, punishment philosophy)

argument_building_framework.description: For every article ingested, the system should extract these dimensions.

dimensions.name: THE CLAIM
dimensions.question: What is the core argument this article is making?

dimensions.name: THE WARRANT
dimensions.question: What evidence or reasoning supports it?

dimensions.name: THE IMPACT
dimensions.question: Who is affected and how much does it matter?

dimensions.name: THE CLASH
dimensions.question: What value or fact does the opposing side contest?

dimensions.name: THE COMPARATIVE
dimensions.question: How does another country or time period handle this differently?

dimensions.name: THE STEELMAN
dimensions.question: What is the strongest version of the argument you disagree with?

dimensions.name: THE BLIND SPOT
dimensions.question: What does this article not address that a good rebuttal would target?

weekly_knowledge_filter.description: Rules for the Weekend Agent when deciding what to keep forever.
keep_permanently: Named concepts with an author or origin (intersectionality, Overton window, Thucydides Trap)
Philosophical frameworks and what they predict (Rawls says X, Nozick says Y)
Historical events that keep getting cited in debates
Statistics that are stable over time (incarceration rates, gender pay gap methodology)
Argument patterns by name (tu quoque, slippery slope, burden of proof)
Value hierarchies — when liberty clashes with equality, who wins and why
Definitions that are disputed — controlling the definition often wins the debate
discard_permanently: Specific election results with no broader systemic lesson
Corporate earnings or stock market movements
Celebrity controversies with no policy dimension
News events that are purely procedural with no normative angle

debate_skill_targets.description: Beyond content, the system should help build these specific debate skills.

skills.how: For every topic, identify the 2-3 words whose definition controls the debate outcome
Always ask — who benefits, who loses, who is invisible in this framing
Identify who carries the burden in each motion and whether that is fair
Name the two values in tension and argue why yours should win in this context
Have at least one country or historical example ready per argument
Learn to grant the opponent's point partially and show why it does not defeat your case
Argue probability, magnitude, and reversibility — not just that harm exists but how bad it is
